coredata.dc:title: Carbon-dot hydrogels as superior carbonaceous adsorbents for removing perfluorooctane sulfonate from water
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Wei-Rong
Chen, Pei-Yao
Deng, Jie
Chen, Yu
Liu, Hua-Ji
coredata.dc:description: Efficiently removing perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) from wastewaters is a hot topic in environmental field. Herein, crosslinking the nano-sized and zero-dimensional carbon dots (CDs) with polyethylene glycol diglycidyl ether (PEG) and polypropylene glycol diglycidyl ether (PPG) resulted in CD hydrogels that were verified by TEM, SEM, FTIR and XPS. The obtained CD hydrogels could efficiently adsorb PFOS from water through their CD moieties. The kinetic curves were fitted well by the pseudo-second order model, indicating the involvement of chemisorption in the rate-limiting step. The adsorption isotherm measurements showed that the hydrogel with the highest CD content had a PFOS maximum uptake capacity of ca. 2.82 g/g (5.64 mmol/g) in the neutral water, which was much higher than the ever-reported carbonaceous materials, and also ranked the top three among the superior positively-charged adsorbents. The superior PFOS adsorption of CD hydrogels could be attributed to good dispersion of the nanoscale CDs in the PEG/PPG hydrogel matrix, leading to the substantial exposure of the binding sites of CDs towards PFOS molecules. Moreover, CD hydrogels did not show pronounced loss of adsorption capacity after experiencing five adsorption-desorption cycles. As for the real wastewaters, CD hydrogels could efficiently treat not only the high concentration of PFOS in the neutral firefighting wastewaters, but also the extremely low concentration of PFOS in the neutral environmental wastewater, where the residual PFOS concentration could be reduced less than 5 ng/L, satisfying the standard of the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Adsorption
Carbon dot
Hydrogel
Perfluorooctane sulfonate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of the pH of point of zero charge (pHpzc)
Preparation and characterization of CD hydrogels

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Water adsorption properties of CD-E/P hydrogels with different CD content; (B) Typical FT-IR spectra of CD-E/P hydrogels and their precursors of CD, PEG and PPG.
Typical XPS spectra of CD and CD-E/P hydrogels (CD37-0.2E/P as the representative).
Typical SEM images of CD-E/P hydrogels with a different CD content.

PFOS adsorption kinetics by 100 mg/L of CD-E/P hydrogel (A) at different pH ([PFOS]0 = 45 mg/L; CD28-0.2E/P was used); (B) at different initial concentration of PFOS (CD28-0.2E/P was used at pH 7.0); (C) with different CD content at pH 7.0 ([PFOS]0 = 50 mg/L); (D) with different PEG/PPG ratio at pH 7.0 ([PFOS]0 = 40 mg/L). All the curves were fitted by pseudo second-order model.
Typical XPS spectra of PFOS-adsorbed CD hydrogel: (A) C 1 s, (B) N 1 s, (C) O 1 s and (D) F1s.
Influence of (A) pH, (B) [PFOS]0, (C) CD content in the hydrogel, and (D) E/P ratio in the hydrogel on the parameters fitted from the pseudo second order kinetic model.

PFOS adsorption isotherm curves of CD-E/P hydrogels (A) with different PEG/PPG ratio; and (B) with different CD content (50 mg/L of hydrogel was used; pH 7.0; 48 h). Curves were fitted with Langmuir (solid line) and Freundlich (dash line) models.

(A) PFBS, PFHxS and PFOS adsorption isotherm curves of CD66-0.2E/P hydrogel (50 mg/L of hydrogel was used; pH 7.0; 48 h), and (B) Interpreted q m values from the corresponding adsorption isotherm curves.

PFOS removal efficiency of CD66-0.2E/P (A) in water spiked with SDS, B12, DG and BE ([PFOS]0 = 20 mg/L; 25 mg/L of hydrogel; 24 h); and (B) in AFFF wastewaters ([PFOS]0 = 20 mg/L; 25 mg/L of hydrogel; 24 h).

Time-dependent residual concentration of low concentration of PFOS ([PFOS]0 = 518.4 ng/L) and PFOS removal efficiency in simulated environmental wastewater system (200 mg/L of NaCl and 20 mg/L of humic acid at pH 6.4) with the two-stage addition of CD66-0.2E/P adsorbent (25 mg/L + 25 mg/L).
Preparation of CD hydrogel and its application as adsorbent for PFOS removal.

head.ce:title: Carbon-dot hydrogels as superior carbonaceous adsorbents for removing perfluorooctane sulfonate from water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300354, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300354, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Fire Research Institute of MEM, Tianjin 300381, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Fire Research Institute of MEM

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tianjin Fire Research Institute of MEM, Tianjin 300381, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Engineering Technology Center of Chemical Wastewater Source Reduction and Recycling, School of Science, Tianjin Chengjian University, Tianjin 300384, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Engineering Technology Center of Chemical Wastewater Source Reduction and Recycling

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tianjin Engineering Technology Center of Chemical Wastewater Source Reduction and Recycling, School of Science, Tianjin Chengjian University, Tianjin 300384, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300354, PR China (Y. Chen).

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: W-R W and P-Y C contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Efficiently removing perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) from wastewaters is a hot topic in environmental field. Herein, crosslinking the nano-sized and zero-dimensional carbon dots (CDs) with polyethylene glycol diglycidyl ether (PEG) and polypropylene glycol diglycidyl ether (PPG) resulted in CD hydrogels that were verified by TEM, SEM, FTIR and XPS. The obtained CD hydrogels could efficiently adsorb PFOS from water through their CD moieties. The kinetic curves were fitted well by the pseudo-second order model, indicating the involvement of chemisorption in the rate-limiting step. The adsorption isotherm measurements showed that the hydrogel with the highest CD content had a PFOS maximum uptake capacity of ca. 2.82 g/g (5.64 mmol/g) in the neutral water, which was much higher than the ever-reported carbonaceous materials, and also ranked the top three among the superior positively-charged adsorbents. The superior PFOS adsorption of CD hydrogels could be attributed to good dispersion of the nanoscale CDs in the PEG/PPG hydrogel matrix, leading to the substantial exposure of the binding sites of CDs towards PFOS molecules. Moreover, CD hydrogels did not show pronounced loss of adsorption capacity after experiencing five adsorption-desorption cycles. As for the real wastewaters, CD hydrogels could efficiently treat not only the high concentration of PFOS in the neutral firefighting wastewaters, but also the extremely low concentration of PFOS in the neutral environmental wastewater, where the residual PFOS concentration could be reduced less than 5 ng/L, satisfying the standard of the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency.

ce:section.ce:para: Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) are normally high-value chemical products, which exhibit irreplaceable functions in some fields due to their special physical properties, such as high chemical and thermal stability, superior surface-tension-lowering properties [1,2]. Perfluorooctane sulfonic acid (PFOS) is one of the most studied PFASs because of the excellent surface activity of itself and its derivatives [3]. At environmental pH PFOS exists in the dissociated form due to its strong acidity [4], therefore it is also referred to as perfluorooctane sulfonate. Unfortunately, PFOS is extremely persistent in the environment, and also exhibits toxicity to humans [5,6]. Finally, PFOS and its salts were categorized as persistent organic pollutants (POPs) by the 4th conference of Stockholm Convention in May 2009 [7]. Until now, the use of PFOS has been only permitted in a few limited fields, such as chrome plating and firefighting foam during the exemption period in developing countries [8].

The released high concentrations of PFOS from factories or firefighting activities can contaminate soil, surface water and groundwater [9-12]. For treating the wastewaters containing high concentrations of PFOS, the simple adsorption technology attracted more attention and versatile adsorption materials were presented for PFOS removal [13,14]. Among so many versatile adsorption materials, carbonaceous materials formed a big family [15], such as the commercially-available powdered activated carbon (PAC) and granular activated carbon (GAC) [16-18], activated carbon fiber [19], biochar[20,21], single-walled carbon nanotube [21,22], multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWNT) [21], magnetic reduced graphene oxide [23,24]. Until now, a few non-carbonaceous materials including anion-exchange resins [25-27], crosslinked chitosan beads [28], aminated polyacrylonitrile fibers [29], and porous aromatic frameworks [30] have showed superior PFOS adsorption capacities of more than 2000 mg/g, especially under acidic condition. However, all the common carbonaceous materials only possess a PFOS adsorption capacity of less than 1000 mg/g. The best carbonaceous material is single-walled carbon nanotube that has a PFOS adsorption capacity of 712 mg/g [21]. It is clear that a big performance gap exists between carbonaceous materials and these several high-performance non-carbonaceous materials. Effectively enhancing the PFOS adsorption capacities of carbonaceous materials is still a big challenge.

Carbon dots (CDs), as the nano-sized, zero-dimensional carbon materials, possess the properties of photoluminescence, low cost, simple synthesis and low toxicity [31]. Recently several CD-based materials including CD-modified magnetic nanocomposites [32-36], CD modified mesoporous organosilica [37], poly(ethylene terephthalate)/MWNT/CD nanocomposite [38], and CD modified polyacrylonitrile fibers [39] have been attempted in the field of adsorbing organic pollutants, such as benzo[a]pyrene [32], carbamazepine[33], methyl orange [34,39], tetracycline [35], methyl blue [36,38], 2,4-dichlorophenol [37]. The best adsorption capacity was achieved by CD-modified magnetic nanocomposites for tetracycline with an adsorption capacity of 592 mg/g [35].

Carbonaceous materials were good adsorbents for many kinds of pollutants, however, the serious agglomeration and the big size of the ever-used carbonaceous materials might severely reduce or block their adsorption sites. On the basis of the fact that the one-dimensional single-walled carbon nanotube has the best PFOS adsorption performance among so many carbonaceous materials [21], it may be deduced that reducing the size of carbonaceous materials is an effective way to enhance their adsorption performance. Compared with the one-dimensional single-walled carbon nanotube, the nano-sized, zero-dimensional CDs have higher surface area, which implies that CDs might have more adsorption sites. In order to restrict the aggregation of CDs and sufficiently expose their adsorption sites, dispersing CDs into a matrix might help. Based on the above deductions, in this work, we designed a kind of CD hydrogels, where the CD entities were dispersed well in the hydrogel matrix. The as-prepared CD hydrogels were used for adsorbing PFOS. To our surprise, the optimized CD hydrogels could adsorb PFOS with the maximal adsorption capacity of ca. 2.82 g/g even in the neutral water, which was almost four-fold higher than the best carbonaceous material of single-walled carbon nanotube, seven-fold higher than the practically used GAC [16,17], and ranked the top three among the superior positively-charged adsorbents. Moreover, it was found that CD hydrogels could efficiently treat not only the high concentration of PFOS in the neutral firefighting wastewaters, but also the extremely low concentration of PFOS in the neutral environmental wastewater.

Perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS, potassium salt), perfluorohexane sulfonate (PFHxS, potassium salt) and perfluorobutane sulfonate (PFBS, potassium salt) were purchased from J&K. Dodecyl glucoside (DG), N-dodecyl-N,N-dimethylglycine (B12), sodium dodecylsulfate (SDS) and 2-(2-butoxyethoxy) ethanol (BE) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Two aqueous film-forming foam (AFFF) concentrates without fluorosurfactants were provided by Jiangsu Suolong Fire Science & Technology Co., Ltd and Shanghai Liuli Fire Technology Co., Ltd, respectively. The corresponding firefighting wastewaters from these two AFFFs spiked with specific amount of PFOS were named as AFFF-1 and AFFF-2, respectively. CD was prepared according to the previous publication [39]. Polyethylene glycol diglycidyl ether (PEG, epoxy equivalent = 278–360) and polypropylene glycol diglycidyl ether (PPG, epoxy equivalent = 278–360) were purchased from Shanghai Rufa Chemical Technology Company.

CDx-yE/P and CD-E/P represents the CD hydrogel that is formed from CD, PEG and PPG, and the hydrogel has a CD weight percentage of × and a y weight ratio of PEG to PPG. CDx-E represents the CD- hydrogel that is formed from CD and PEG. CDx-P represents the CD hydrogel that is formed from CD and PPG.

Using CD66-0.2E/P as an example, CD (1.00 g), PEG (0.162 g) and PPG (0.807) were mixed together in 8.60 mL of methanol. The mixture was put in an orbital shaker at 200 rpm and 25 °C, and gel was gradually formed following the time elapse. After two days. The obtained gel was washed with a vast amount of methanol to remove the unreacted PEG and PPG, and then dried in a vacuum desiccator at 180 °C for 2 days to remove the solvent. Yield: 77.1%.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out with a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi + photoelectron spectrometer using a monochromatic Al Kα radiation at 250 W. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were obtained on a Nicolet 360 FT-IR spectrometer. The surfaces of the hydrogels were measured by scanning electron microscope (SEM, Phenom Prox). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) study was recorded by a JEOL JEM-2100F instrument. PFOS concentrations in solution were determined by HPLC-MS/MS system (Agilent 1290–6460) with a triple quadrupole mass spectrometer, and the detailed operation parameters were shown in supporting information (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the pH of point of zero charge (pHpzc)
ce:section.ce:para: Adsorbent (50 mg) and NaCl aq (0.1 M, 10 mL) were added to the centrifuge tube, and then the initial pH of the solution was adjusted to 2–11 by HCl aq or NaOH aq. The centrifuge tube was shaken at 25℃ with 200 rpm. After 48 h, the shaking was stopped, and the adsorbent therefore settled. Then the final pH of the supernatant was recorded. If the pH value of the solution does not change after adding the adsorbent, choose this pH value as pHpzc of the adsorbent.

All PFOS adsorption experiments were conducted in an orbital shaker at 200 rpm and 25 °C. In order to obtain the best pH environment for PFOS adsorption, 5.0 mg of ground gel was put into 50 mL of PFOS aqueous solution. The pH value of the mixture was adjusted by adding HCl aq or NaOH aq. Sorption isotherm experiments were conducted at initial PFOS concentrations ranging from 6 to 460 mg/L over 48 h. 5.0 mg of gel was soaked into 100 mL of the PFOS aqueous solution at pH 7.0. The concentration of residual PFOS in solution was analyzed by HPLC-MS/MS, and each adsorption experiment was run in duplicate, and the average value was adopted.

Interference of hydrocarbon surfactants or organic solvent with the PFOS adsorption: A series of binary aqueous solutions of PFOS with SDS, B12, DG or BE were prepared. The concentration of PFOS was fixed to be 20 mg/L, whereas the concentrations of hydrocarbon surfactants or organic solvent varied from 20, 100, 200, 1000 to 2000 mg/L. Subsequently, 5.0 mg of ground gel was put into 200 mL of the binary solution, and the mixture was shaken at 200 rpm and 25 °C for 24 h. After taking out the gel, the residual PFOS in solution was analyzed by HPLC-MS/MS. The similar procedure was adopted by the PFOS adsorption in the AFFF wastewaters.

PFOS adsorption in environmental wastewater: Simulated environmental wastewater was prepared, which was composed of 518.4 ng/L of PFOS, 200 mg/L of NaCl and 20 mg/L of humic acid at pH 6.4. Then 5.0 mg of ground gel was put into 200 mL of the simulated environmental wastewater, and the mixture was shaken at 200 rpm and 25 °C. The time-dependent residual concentration of PFOS in the wastewater was monitored by HPLC-MS/MS. One hour later, the PFOS-adsorbed gel was taken out, and then 5.0 mg of ground fresh gel was put into the wastewater again for the further adsorption.

PFOS desorption and gel reuse: The tea bag containing 5.0 mg of gel was soaked in PFOS aqueous solution (100 mL, 34 mg/L) and shaken in an orbital shaker at 25 °C for 6 h. Subsequently, the gel was taken out and dried with filter paper. The dried gel was put into tea bag, and was then soaked in 50 mL of methanol for 1 h. The gel was taken out and dried at 50 °C under vacuum for 6 h. Five adsorption-desorption cycles are carried out. HPLC-MS/MS was used to monitor the residual amount of PFOS in water and the release amount of PFOS in methanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CD hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: CD capped with amine groups was prepared from hyperbranched polyethylenimine (PEI) and citric acid (CA) according to the previous publication [39]. The resulting carbon particles were amorphous, and their average diameters were ca. 5 nm (Fig. S1). Through the addition reaction between the multi-amine groups of CD and the diepoxide groups of PEG/PPG, CD-E/P hydrogel was formed (Scheme 1 ). By adjusting the feed ratio of CD/PEG/PPG, a series of hydrogels with CD content in the range of 28–66% were prepared. Through varying the PEG/PPG ratio, the hydrophilicity of the obtained hydrogels was adjusted. In the TEM image of CD-E/P hydrogel, the dark dots coming from the CD moieties can be seen clearly (Fig. S1), indicating the successful preparation of CD-E/P hydrogels.

Transparent thin plates with smooth surface can be shaped from the obtained hydrogels (Fig. S2). It is found that the hydrogel plate with a higher content of CD is more elastic. Moreover, the hydrogel with a higher content of CD possesses a higher water adsorption capacity (Fig. 1 A), which points out the higher hydrophilicity of CD moieties than PEG/PPG. The smooth hydrogel plates become wrinkled after being taken out from the pure water or PFOS aqueous solution, which can restore their original state after drying at 50 °C for 2 h (Fig. S3).

The obtained hydrogels were characterized by FT-IR (Fig. 1B). Compared with the precursors of CD and PEG/PPG, it is clear that CD hydrogels possess both the unique bands of CD and PEG/PPG. For instance, CD has the unique absorption bands as follows: (1) The broad strong band centered at ca. 3285 cm−1 includes the stretching vibration of secondary amides and the combination of NH symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of primary amines; (2) The strong band at ca. 1647 cm−1 is originated from the NH in-plane deformation vibration of primary amines and the CO stretching vibration of secondary amides; (3) The strong band at ca. 1553 cm−1 is the combination of NH deformation and C-N stretching vibrations of secondary amides. As for PEG/PPG, their unique absorption bands are located at 2975 cm−1 and 2869 cm−1 that are typical CH3 asymmetric stretching vibrations. The strongest absorption band at ca. 1100 cm−1 in hydrogels can ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C-O-C of PEG/PPG, C-N of CD, and secondary alcohol (C-OH) from the ring-opening of epoxides of PEG/PPG due to the attacking of amine groups of CD. Overall, the FT-IR spectra further prove the successful preparation of CD hydrogels.

The surface elements in the CD-E/P hydrogels were acquired from XPS (Fig. 2 ), which were compared with the CD precursor. In both XPS spectra of CD and CD-E/P, signals of C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s can be observed clearly (Fig. 2A). CD-E/P possesses more O elements, but less N elements than CD, which can be ascribed to the successful grafting of O-rich PEG/PPG onto the N-rich CD. Fig. 2B shows the intense C 1 s signals of CD and CD-E/P. Three peaks with binding energy (BE) at ca. 284.2, 285.1 and 287.1 eV are obtained through deconvolution from the raw C 1 s signal of CD, which can be assigned as the aliphatic carbons connected to amines (15.6%), the aromatic carbons (79.6%), and the amide carbons (4.8%), respectively. The C 1 s signal of CD-E/P can be also deconvoluted into three similar peaks, where the contents of aromatic carbons (49.8 %) and the amide carbons (2.6%) decrease markedly, whereas the peak at ca. 284.2 eV (47.6%) becomes intensified due to the introduction of aliphatic carbons from PEG/PPG moieties. Since nitrogen atoms only come from CD, the N 1 s spectra of CD and CD-E/P are similar, and both contain the typical amine and amide nitrogen (Fig. 2C). From the O 1 s spectra (Fig. 2D), it is clear that CD only has two kinds of oxygen atoms, i.e. oxidized amine (530.3 eV) and amide (531.8 eV), whereas CD-E/P has the extra ether and hydroxyl oxygen atoms from PEG/PPG moieties (532.3 eV). The above characterizations fully verified the successful preparation of CD-E/P hydrogels.

The obtained CD-E/P hydrogels were further characterized by SEM (Fig. 3 ). It is clear that the surface of CD66-E/P with the high content of CD is very rough and granules can be observed in the magnified images. With the gradual decreasing of CD content, the surface of CD-E/P hydrogels become more and more smooth due to the increase of the soft PEG/PPG moieties.

The PFOS-adsorbed CD hydrogels were characterized by FT-IR (Fig. S4). Compared with the spectrum of CD hydrogel without the uptake of PFOS, it is clear that new adsorption bands occur at 1242, 1207 and 1149 cm−1, which are the typical CF stretching vibration bands of polyfluorinated compounds. The SO symmetric stretching band of PFOS occur at 1092 cm−1, which overlaps with the adsorption bands of CD hydrogel. The broad adsorption band centered at 3412 cm−1 can be assigned as the OH stretching vibration of sulfonic acid group of PFOS. After all the spectra are normalized using the CH3 asymmetric stretching vibration at 2869 cm−1 of PPG moieties, it is obvious that the PFOS-related adsorption bands are strengthened following the quantity increase of adsorbed PFOS, whereas the CD hydrogel related adsorption bands become weakened.

The PFOS-adsorbed CD hydrogels were also characterized by XPS. In the whole XPS spectra (Fig. S5), intense signals of F 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s and C 1 s can be seen clearly. Since CD hydrogels only possess C, H, O and N element, the occurrence of the intense F 1 s signal supports the successful adsorption of PFOS by CD hydrogels. Fig. 5 A shows the typical C 1 s signal of PFOS-adsorbed CD hydrogel. Through deconvolution from the raw C 1 s signal, five peaks with BE at ca. 284.5, 285.3, 286.2, 287.5 and 291.8 eV are obtained. Compared with the C 1 s signal of CD hydrogel (Fig. 2A), it is clear that the two peaks at BEs of 286.2 and 291.8 eV are new. The peak at BE = 291.8 eV is assigned as the perfluoroalkyl carbons, while the peak at BE = 286.2 eV can be assigned as the carbons connected to the protonated amines of CD hydrogel that are generated during the uptake of PFOS. The generation of protonated amines can be also verified by the N 1 s spectrum of PFOS-adsorbed CD hydrogel (Fig. 5B), where a new peak at BE = 401.6 eV can be seen through comparison with the N 1 s spectrum of CD hydrogel (Fig. 2B). In the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 5C), besides the typical peaks from CD hydrogel (Fig. 2C), the peak from the sulfonic acid group of PFOS can be found at BE = 532.8 eV. Fig. 5D shows the intense F 1 s signal. Three peaks with BEs at ca. 688.0, 689.0 and 689.8 eV are obtained through deconvolution from the raw F 1 s signal, which can be assigned as the –CF2– connected to sulfonamide, the middle –CF2–, and the terminal–CF3, respectively, of the adsorbed PFOS. Overall, the FT-IR and XPS characterizations prove that CD hydrogels can effectively adsorb PFOS molecules.

Subsequently, all the kinetic curves were fitted by both pseudo first-order [Eq. (1)] [40] and pseudo second-order [Eq. (2)] [41] adsorption models, and the fitted parameters are summarized in Table S2.
(1)
q t
=
q e
1 - exp
-
k 1
t
Pseudo
- f i r s t
- o r d e r
(2)
q t
= h t /
1
+
k 2
q e
t
Pseudo
- sec o n d
- o r d e r
Where k 1 is adsorption rate constant; t is adsorption time; q e and q t are equilibrium and real-time adsorption amount, respectively; k 2 is the overall rate constant; h (h = k 2 q e 2) is the initial adsorption rate constant.

For all the kinetic curves, the correlation coefficients (R2) using the pseudo second-order model are more than 0.95. R2 more than 0.95 can be also acquired through using the pseudo first-order model in most of the cases except under the acidic condition. For instance, at pH 3.0, the R2 value using the pseudo first-order model is only 0.884. It is known that pseudo first order kinetic model assumes that rate of adsorption is proportional to the number of free binding sites, while pseudo second order kinetic model assumes that rate of adsorption is proportional to the square of the number of unoccupied sites due to the involvement of chemisorption. CD-E/P hydrogels contain some amine groups that can interact with the anionic PFOS (pKa = −3.27) [4] through ionic interaction. This chemisorption factor results in the good matching of the pseudo second order kinetic model with all the kinetic curves. When the solution pH is set to be neutral, the number of protonated amine groups are reduced markedly compared with that under acidic condition, which decreases the chemisorption contribution to the rate-limiting step. Hence, under neutral condition, the kinetic curves can be also fitted well by the pseudo first order adsorption model.

The effects of the initial adsorption conditions on the parameters interpreted from the pseudo second order kinetic model were shown in Fig. 6 . With the gradual increase of the solution pH, both the overall adsorption rate constant, k 2, and the initial adsorption rate constant, h, decrease continuously (Fig. 6A). This can be ascribed to the gradual decrease of the protonated amine groups, which leads to the decrease of chemisorption of PFOS. The equilibrium adsorption amount, q e, is similar in the pH range of 3–9, but deceases markedly at pH 11. The pHpzc of CD-E/P is measured to be a little higher than 9. Hence at pH 11 all the amine groups of CD-E/P are in the neutral state, which lose the capability of chemically adsorbing PFOS molecules. Consequently, this results in the marked q e decrease.

From Fig. 6B it can be seen that the high [PFOS]0 can efficiently raise the q e value and accelerate the adsorption rate. As for the adsorbents, the influence of CD content on q e is complex, and higher k 2 and h are achieved by hydrogel with a middle content of CD (Fig. 6C). This implies that the balance of the CD moieties and PEG/PPG moieties decides the transporting rate of PFOS molecules to the surface of CD moieties. From Fig. 6D and Table S2 it can be known that suitable ratio of PEG/PPG in CD-E/P hydrogel leads to a higher k 2, h and q e than the pure CD-E and CD-P hydrogels. PFOS is an amphiphilic molecule that has a higher affinity to the amphiphilic PEG/PPG moieties than to the pure hydrophilic PEG zones and the pure relatively hydrophobic PPG zones. Hence, the concerted functions of PEG and PPG facilitate the transporting of PFOS molecules to the CD moieties.

The kinetic curves were further fitted by Weber-Morris intraparticle diffusion model [Eq. (3)] [42] and Elovich model [Eq. (4)] [20].
q t
=
k
dif
t
0.5
+ C
Weber - M o r r i s
int r a p a r t i c l e
d i f f u s i o n
q t
= β ln
α / β
+ β ln t
Elovich
Where k dif is the diffusion rate coefficient; α is the initial adsorption constant, β is the desorption rate.

The kinetic curves fitted by Weber-Morris intraparticle diffusion model show very poor R2 values in the range of 0.382–0.853 (Table S3), further verifying that CD-E/P hydrogels are not porous materials. It is known that the Elovich model is mainly applicable to the initial stage of the adsorption process, which is relatively far from equilibrium. The kinetic curves fitted by Elovich model have R2 values ranging from 0.784 to 0.976 (Table S3). The overall R2 values are not so good enough that the surface of CD-E/P hydrogels cannot be regarded to be energetically heterogeneous.

The adsorption isotherm measurements could provide information of the maximum uptake capacities (q m) of adsorbents for adsorbates. PFOS-containing firefighting or environmental wastewaters are normally neutral, thus the isotherm adsorptions of CD-E/P hydrogels for PFOS were measured at pH 7.0 (Fig. 7 ). Initially, q e increases significantly with the increase of PFOS concentration, but at last it reaches the plateau. The variation of PEG/PPG ratio in the CD-E/P hydrogels has no marked influence on q m (Fig. 7A), whereas raising the CD content in the CD-E/P hydrogels leads to the pronounced q m enhancement (Fig. 7B). This further proves that CD is the real binding sites of PFOS molecules, not the PEG/PPG moieties. Using CD66-E/P as the adsorbent, q m can reach ca. 2.82 g/g (5.64 mmol/g), which is much higher than the ever-reported carbonaceous materials (Table S4). For instance, the best carbonaceous material, single-walled carbon nanotube has only a q m of 712 mg/g for PFOS [21], while the commercially-available, cheap PAC and GAC have a q m of 520 mg/g and 390 mg/g for PFOS, respectively [16,17]. Different from the pure carbonaceous materials, CD-E/P hydrogels still contain some amine groups that can be protonated under suitable pH condition, which also help their capture of the anionic PFOS through the electrostatic attraction interaction. Compared with the other positively-charged adsorbents (Table S5) [25-29,43-49], CD66-E/P is only inferior to the up-to-date best PFOS adsorbent, aminated polyacrylonitrile fiber, that has a q m of 7.49 g/g [29], but shows a comparable PFOS adsorption capacity to the second-ranked commercial anion-exchange resin IRA67 that has a q m of 2.84 g/g [27] or 2.57 g/g [26]. Overall, the superior PFOS adsorption of CD66-E/P might be attributed to good dispersion of the nanoscale CDs in the PEG/PPG hydrogel matrix, which leads to the substantial exposure of the binding sites of CDs towards PFOS molecules. Finally, the concerted actions of electrostatic attraction and hydrophobic interaction between CD moieties and PFOS molecules result in the efficient PFOS adsorption.

For further learning how PFOS interacts with CD-E/P hydrogel, the equilibrium adsorption curves in Fig. 7 are fitted by the well-known Langmuir [50] and Freundlich [51] isotherm models, and they are expressed as Eq. (5) and Eq. (6), respectively.
(5)
q
e
=
q
m
K
d
C
e
1 +
K
d
C
e
( L a n g m u i r )
(6)
q
e
=
K
F
C
e
1 / n
( F r e u n d l i c h )
where C e is the equilibrium concentration of PFOS; Kd is the Langmuir constant related to rate of adsorption, respectively. K F and n are the respective Freundlich isotherm constants related to adsorption capacity and adsorption intensity.

The fittings by Langmuir model show R2 values in the range of 0.958–0.982, better than the R2 values (0.927–0.958) fitted by Freundlich model (Table S6). Thus, it is clear that the adsorption of PFOS by CD-E/P hydrogel mainly satisfies the Langmuir adsorption mechanism that assumes a homogeneous adsorbent surface with identical sorption sites.

Subsequently, the possibility of the regeneration and reuse of PFOS-adsorbed CD-E/P hydrogel was studied. Methanol was used as the desorbing solvent. It is found than more than 90% of PFOS can be desorbed, and the regenerated CD-E/P hydrogel can be reused to effectively adsorb PFOS again (Fig. S6). After five adsorption/desorption cycle experiments, the PFOS adsorption performance of CD-E/P hydrogel does not show marked loss, pointing out that CD-E/P hydrogel is robust enough for the PFOS adsorption.

PFOS-containing firefighting wastewaters can be generated through the utilization of aqueous film-forming foam (AFFF) fire extinguishing agents. The formulations of commercial AFFFs are complex proprietary mixtures, however, they contain at least four types of ingredients – fluorosurfactants, hydrocarbon surfactants, cosolvents and water [11,52]. After communicating with the fire extinguishing agent manufacturers, SDS, B12, DG, BE were selected as the typical hydrocarbon surfactants or organic solvents. SDS, B12 and DG are anionic, zwitterionic and nonionic surfactants, respectively. BE is an organic solvent that improves the compatibility of the components in the AFFF concentrate formulation. First, how these hydrocarbon surfactants and organic solvents interfere with the PFOS removal efficiency were studied (Fig. 9 A). With CD66-0.2E/P (25 mg/L) as the adsorbent, the PFOS removal efficiency is lowered only ca. 2% in the presence of the same amount of SDS as PFOS, whereas it is enhanced a little in the presence of the same amount of B12, DG or BE. After raising the concentrations of these four compounds in the range of 5–100 fold higher than PFOS, only BE does good to the PFOS removal no matter what its concentration is, whereas the other hydrocarbon surfactants gradually interfere with the PFOS removal following the increase of their concentrations. The interfering degree is in the ordering of SDS > DG > B12. The negative effect exhibited by SDS can be attributed to its anionic sulfate group that competes with the anionic sulfonate group of PFOS to interact with the positively-charged ammonium groups of CDs. As for the nonionic DG, when its concentration reaches the 10-fold higher than PFOS, its hydrocarbon chain starts to exhibit obvious competition with the perfluoroalkyl chain of PFOS to interact with the hydrophobic CD moieties of hydrogel. The polar zwitterionic group B12 is composed of the positive quaternary amine and the negative carboxylate. Carboxylic acid is medium acid, and under neutral condition only partial carboxylic acids are in the dissociated state. Thus, B12 exhibits more positive character, which leads to the electrostatic repulsion with the positively-charged ammonium groups of CDs. Consequently, the interference of B12 in the concentration range of 1–10 fold higher than PFOS is negligible. CD66-0.2E/P hydrogel was subsequently applied for adsorbing the PFOS in the real firefighting wastewaters due to the utilization of AFFF fire extinguishing agents. From Fig. 9B it is clear that CD66-0.2E/P has a similar or a little higher PFOS removal efficiencies in AFFF-1 and AFFF-2 wastewaters than in pure water. This demonstrates that the formulations of these AFFF extinguishing agents might not contain the anionic surfactants. Moreover, according to the rough AFFF formulation (Buckeye 3%, Buckeye Fire Equipment Company, Inc.) widely used foam for naval firefighting applications [53], it is known that the concentrations of hydrocarbon surfactants are normally less than 6-fold higher than fluorosurfactant, whereas the concentration of organic solvent is in the range of 6–40 fold higher than fluorosurfactant. From Fig. 9A it is known that in such concentration range, the interfering effects of hydrocarbon surfactants are weak, which can be compensated by the positive effect of organic solvent in AFFF wastewaters.

In environmental waters the concentration of PFOS is normally very low. Thus, CD66-0.2E/P was further attempted for treating the low-concentration of PFOS (518.4 ng/L) in simulated wastewater that contained 200 mg/L of NaCl and 20 mg/L of humic acid at pH 6.4 (Fig. 10 ). The fresh 25 mg/L of CD66-0.2E/P can remove ca. 86.6% PFOS within half an hour, and the concentration of residual PFOS is ca. 69.7 ng/L. Further prolonging the adsorption time does not help. After an hour, The PFOS-loaded CD66-0.2E/P was removed, and then another fresh 25 mg/L of CD66-0.2E/P was added. It was found that the PFOS removal efficiency reached ca. 95.5% within half an hour and the residual concentration of PFOS dropped to 23 ng/L, which is much lower than the highest level of 70 ng/L in drinking water permitted by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency [54]. Furthermore, PFOS cannot be detected again (limit of detection is ca. 5 ng/L) after 2 h, pointing out the excellent adsorption performance of CD66-0.2E/P for PFOS removal in environmental wastewater.

CD hydrogels were successfully prepared through the crosslinking reaction among the nano-sized and zero-dimensional CD, PEG and PPG. CD hydrogels could be used as adsorbents for PFOS removal from water, and the CD entities of hydrogels were the real residing sites of PFOS molecules. The adsorption kinetic curves satisfied the pseudo-second order model, indicating the involvement of chemisorption in the rate-limiting step. While the adsorption isotherm curves satisfied the Langmuir mechanism, demonstrating that CD-hydrogels had homogeneous surface with identical sorption sites. Raising the CD content favored the enhancement of q m for PFOS, the hydrogel with the highest CD content had a q m of ca. 2.82 g/g (5.64 mmol/g) for PFOS in the neutral water, which was much higher than the ever-reported carbonaceous materials, and also ranked the top three among the positively-charged superior PFOS adsorbents. This work opened up a new way to markedly enhance the adsorption performance of carbonaceous materials for PFOS. CD hydrogels could be easily regenerated by washing with methanol and reused. Furthermore, CD hydrogels could efficiently treat not only the neutral firefighting wastewaters containing high concentration of PFOS, but also the neutral environmental wastewaters with extremely low concentration of PFOS. The residual PFOS concentration in wastewaters could be reduced to be less than 5 ng/L, satisfying the standard of the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation, China (No. 21875159).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.135021.

ce:section.ce:para:

H.M.F. Freundlich, Over the adsorption in solution, J. Phys. Chem., 57 (1906) 385-470.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Short-chain and long-chain fluorosurfactants in firefighting foam: A review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Peshoria, D. Nandini, R.K. Tanwar, R. Narang, Short-chain and long-chain fluorosurfactants in firefighting foam: A review, Environ. Chem. Lett., 18 (2020) 1277-1300.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.M. Hinnant, M.W. Conroy, R. Ananth, Influence of fuel on foam degradation for fluorinated and fluorine-free foams, Colloids Surf. A, 522 (2017) 1-17.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.S. Boone, C. Vigo, T. Boone, C. Byrne, J. Ferrario, R. Benson, J. Donohue, J.E. Simmons, D.W. Kolpin, E.T. Furlong, S.T. Glassmeyer, Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances in source and treated drinking waters of the united states, Sci. Total Environ., 653 (2019) 359-369.
